turns.text: Well, there may be. There has been a doctrine of absolute immunity for other executive officers.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Which the Court of Appeals in Yaselli has found?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: Yes. But I think that Scheuer indicates and Wood against Strickland indicates some difference and I am suggesting for executive officers at least in the state context and I am suggesting that difference between that case in Pierson against Ray or Yaselli against Goff is justified by the difference in the process and one of those differences as is, as we said in the Scheuer opinion, I am quoting from Mr. Chief Justice Hughes that where they not judicial review in the damage suit, there would be no way to test the governor’s performance in a courtroom and defy another state governor would be the constitution and not the constitution by the Supreme Law. That is not true here. A prosecutor’s behavior is testable in the courtroom before the jury, it is testable on appeal, it is testable on collateral attack and indeed it is testable ultimately if one gets to that stage upon a petition for commutation of sentence or pardon.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Are you familiar with the Ninth Circuit’s holding in Ronan against Robichaud?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: I do not believe I am Mr. Justice Rehnquist.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, that was a case where the prosecutor had apparently collaborator, who was alleged he collaborated kind of third degree methods in interrogating a witness, would you extend absolute immunity that far?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: There are cases that I think we need not reach for purposes of this case in which a prosecutor does what is regarded as essentially a police function, as in directing a raid or interrogating a witness and it is possible to draw that line. I would be hesitant to draw it very easily because the connection between what he is going to do in the courtroom and what he does outside the courtroom is so close. There may be cases in which that line can be drawn.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: That is so called an investigative function, is it not?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: That is true.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: And at least three circuits have thought there was a distinction?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: And there may be. I do not think we need to address that distinction for the purposes of today’s case.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: And do they, do those decisions say that he then has the immunity of a policeman or he has no immunity at all?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: I think it becomes a qualified immunity I believe.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: The immunity of a policeman if he is acting like a policeman?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Justice Stewart.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: That is what the cases say? I believe so. But the one thing I want to stress here is that in this case, unlike the case of a governor or school official, the prosecutor’s action is subject to repeated review and repeated collateral attack which would not be true if the governor in Scheuer against Rhodes, will not be true if the school officials in Wood against Strickland were not for their not having absolute immunity. It has been mentioned that besides the other safeguards for the defendant, there are sanctions available against a prosecutor which are not available against the governor such as --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: What happens to executive officer who directs a prosecutor to prosecute?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: Well, it is the executive. I mean, a governor who directs an executive officer?

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: To prosecute that would be somewhat difficult case because at that point the governor I take it is exercising his function to make sure the laws are executed in the sense.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: He is acting like the prosecutor?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: He is acting like a prosecutor.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Rather than the prosecutor is acting like a governor?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: That I think is true, but we are talking about a governor calling out the National Guard. We are talking about the situation in which there is no way to get judicial review of that action, if there is not a 1983 suit available.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Is it not true that in most of the states, neither the Governor of the State nor the Attorney General has any final control over the actions of a prosecutor?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: That is I believe true (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: MThe Governor can remove or suspend the prosecutor temporarily for clause, but overwhelmingly the prosecutor elected in a particular jurisdiction is autonomous. Is that not so?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: I think that is quite true Mr. Chief Justice. It may be that my answer to Mr. Justice White was somewhat tempered by my knowledge that the Attorney General can direct prosecution.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: In the federal --

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: In the federal system and our interest is of course in the federal system.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: And even if they can, it often happens in the states they nevertheless listen?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: That may, will be. I am not that familiar with state.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It is a matter of technicality?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: But I think also it is true that judicial process that we are trying to protect with this kind of immunity is a more vulnerable process. It is quite true that prosecutors have to bring many cases and they have antipathy from many defendants and there are categories of defendants who if they were able to sue would do their best to bring the process to a halt or at least the damage of the process by bringing actions.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Solicitor General, I know that your brief has given us a rather comprehensive review of all the Federal Court decisions addressed in this question, but you made no reference to lower or any state court decisions, is there any reason for that?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: No, we did not Justice Brennan because we think at least as to the federal prosecutor’s immunity it is governed by federal law. Now, perhaps I do not know if these gentlemen wish to file the brief, I thought you were asking about this morning.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, it is a good deal of state law?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: Well, shall I ask this gentlemen if they --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: The state decisions would be construing 1983. The only reason we have this case is because it involves a federal question of the construction of a federal statute?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: I think Mr. Justice Brennan is interested more than 1983. He is interested in --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: I am familiar with it.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Well, any state can do whatever he wants about this it is a matter of its own State Tort Law?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: That is true, I think --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Pierson against Ray and those cases called on the historic privilege under common law?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: That is true.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: It is true in 1983?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: But we have --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Yes, but so the question is that -- that may be common law may be of some relevance in the various states?

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Do not let me hold?

turns.name: ROBERT H. BORK
turns.text: Alright I think. (Voice Overlap) It should also be noted that the prosecutor has to establish his case beyond a reasonable doubt to unanimous jury. Should he be reversed on appeal for having done something or be attacked collaterally, he will be sued under a standard by which a majority of the jury may hold him liable on the preponderance of the evidence. I think that is bound to have a deterred effect as the prosecutor decides whether or not to go forward with the case. And finally, I think it may be worth noting that I think it is true that some courts would be a little loathe in a close case to decide if the defendant had been denied his constitutional rights by a prosecutor if that court knew that by doing so he was exposing the prosecutor to a lawsuit for damages. It is not all clear that removing the absolute immunity would help the defendants. It might in fact hurt some defendants on their habeas corpus proceedings. In any event for these reasons we think the absolute immunity which a court has, a judge has, and a juror has properly belongs to a prosecutor as well. There are other sanctions, other safeguards in the judicial process not available elsewhere and for that reason we thank the judgment of the Court of Appeals should be affirmed.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Thank you Mr. Solicitor General. Thank you gentlemen. The case is submitted.
